title: Initialization Rules 🏗️
description: Classes don't build themselves. Learn how to write an init() function.

slides.title: The Missing Freebie 🎁
slides.content:
# No Memberwise Initializer

When we made Structs, Swift generated a free, invisible Initializer for us (e.g. `Player(name: "Tim")`).

Classes are more complex, so Swift purposefully removes this freebie!

You must explicitly write an `init()` method inside your Class to prove to Swift that you know how to build the object.

slides.title: Writing an init() 🛠️

quizData.question: What is the primary function of an `init()` method?

options.text: To crash the code on error.
To safely set the starting values for every Property inside the Class before it is used.

quizData.explanation: Initialization guarantees that when a new object is born, it is fully equipped and ready to be used (no blank or missing variables!).

slides.title: The 'self' Keyword 🪞

quizData.question: In the line `self.score = score`, what does `self` refer to?

options.text: The parameter passed into the init function.
The actual property belonging to the Class object being created.

quizData.explanation: `self` acts like a mirror, pointing the code back toward the Object itself so you can modify its internal state.
